issue_title: RFC3107 support for AF IPv6
issue_body:
Please add RFC3107 support for AF IPv6. RFC 3107 adds the ability to signal labels for non-VPN AFs. 
This RFC doesn't prescribe anything specific to IPv6 and merely extends the NLRI information field with a label stack ahead of the prefix.
Exabgp already contains support for RFC3107 for AF IPv4 thus all the needed functionality to support carrying label information in the NLRI field  appears to already be present but not enabled for AF IPv6.

Relevant RFCs: http://www.ietf.org/rfc/rfc3107.txt

issue_title: Backend process negotiated information on crash / restart
issue_body:
ExaBGP currently can send OPEN, then UPDATE message to the backend process. The OPEN information is required to be able to decode UPDATE ( ASN4, and ADD-PATH negotiation ).

Should the process crash, the process need to have persistently store this data otherwise parsing is impossible.

ExaBGP could help by (both or either):
 - allow the backend process to re-request both send/received OPEN
 - include the OPEN negotiation information in each UPDATE

issue_title: Race condition results in EIP association failing
issue_body:
The EC2 API for associating EIPs appears to be eventually consistent. So if two servers attempt to grab the same EIP at the same time, one of them will succeed and the other will fail but think it succeeded.

Example logs from this happening, with the IP and instance IDs redacted.

```
2017-11-27 19:50:02,225 - aws-ec2-assign-eip - INFO - Connected to AWS EC2 in us-west-2
2017-11-27 19:50:02,668 - aws-ec2-assign-eip - INFO - Successfully associated Elastic IP x.x.x.105 with i-a77fbf
```

```
2017-11-27 19:50:02,064 - aws-ec2-assign-eip - INFO - Connected to AWS EC2 in us-west-2
2017-11-27 19:50:02,909 - aws-ec2-assign-eip - INFO - Successfully associated Elastic IP x.x.x.105 with i-8d7ed1
```

In reality, `i-8d7ed1` won the race and has the EIP associated, while `i-a77fbf` thinks it got the EIP but has no EIP associated.

One way to fix this would be to loop doing the describe-addresses call until the association with the current instance ID appears. Would you be interested in a PR that does this?

issue_title: Add support for Docker v2 registries
issue_body:
I'm here via Saltstack, and troubleshooting a issue with connectivity while doing a pull. The problem is that the library is hardcoded to reference the v1 API to verify SSL connectivity to a local registry, which does not work with v2 registry.

The request should be changed to also try and ping `https:// + hostname + "/v2/"` in addition to `'https://' + hostname + '/v1/_ping'`

The rest of the pull stream works OK, since it is connecting to Docker, not the repo directly otherwise.

See: https://github.com/docker/docker-py/blob/master/docker/auth/auth.py#L32

     def fetch_array_measurement(self, timeout, array_meas_function):

        meas_wfm_ctype = (visatype.ViReal64 * (self._actual_meas_wfm_size() * self._actual_num_wfms()))()  # case 0.2
        wfm_info_ctype = (waveform_info.struct_niScope_wfmInfo * self._actual_num_wfms())()  # case 0.2
        error_code = self._library.niScope_FetchArrayMeasurement(vi_ctype, channel_list_ctype, timeout_ctype, array_meas_function_ctype, meas_wfm_size_ctype, meas_wfm_ctype, wfm_info_ctype)
        errors.handle_error(self, error_code, ignore_warnings=False, is_error_handling=False)
        return [float(meas_wfm_ctype[i]) for i in range((self._actual_meas_wfm_size() * self._actual_num_wfms()))], [waveform_info.WaveformInfo(wfm_info_ctype[i]) for i in range(self._actual_num_wfms())]
```
```
    def _actual_meas_wfm_size(self, array_meas_function):
        if type(array_meas_function) is not enums.ArrayMeasurement:
            raise TypeError('Parameter mode must be of type ' + str(enums.ArrayMeasurement))
        vi_ctype = visatype.ViSession(self._vi)  # case 1

issue_title: READ_ALL_AVAILABLE don't work; in_stream.avail_samp_per_chan always return 0
issue_body:
I spent a good couple of days trying to make the code run with AcquisitionType.CONTINUOUS and READ_ALL_AVAILABLE but nothing seemed to work.

After a lot of time wondering if it was something related to the acquisition, I could finally pinpoint the problem to the read function.

This code works fine:
```
data = None
task = nidaqmx.Task()
task.ai_channels.add_ai_voltage_chan("Dev1/ai1")
task.timing.cfg_samp_clk_timing(rate=60, sample_mode=AcquisitionType.CONTINUOUS)
task.start()
time.sleep(5)
t0=time.ctime()
data = task.read(300)
print "{}\n{}".format(time.ctime(), t0)
task.stop()
task.close()
print data
```
I know the buffer is already filled (and therefore not a problem with the acquisition) because the read function returns instantaneously, without waiting for data, and the data is what I expect.

This, however, don't:

```
data = None
task = nidaqmx.Task()
task.ai_channels.add_ai_voltage_chan("Dev1/ai1")
task.timing.cfg_samp_clk_timing(rate=60, sample_mode=AcquisitionType.CONTINUOUS)
task.start()
time.sleep(5)
t0=time.ctime()
data = task.read(READ_ALL_AVAILABLE)  # ONLY CHANGED FROM 300 to READ_ALL_AVAILABLE
print "{}\n{}".format(time.ctime(), t0)
task.stop()
task.close()
print data
```
And I get an empty list for my `data` variable.

So I read the source code and apparently the avail_samp_per_chan property (function) always return zero, for instance right before the read(300) command:
```
time.sleep(5)
print task.in_stream.avail_samp_per_chan
data = task.read(300)
```

So I believe that it is the problem. This function is not returning the correct number of available samples.

Can you confirm the problem and if so, is there any short-term workaround for this? This is a bit urgent as this is being used in a project that has already blew its deadline.

issue_title: Unclear if ELMo module usable with text_field_embedders?
Allow setting max span length in BiDAF
issue_body:
@OyvindTafjord found that constraining the max span length in a somewhat incorrect way gave a small boost to BiDAF scores.  This was how he did it:

```
+        max_span_length = 17

-                if val1 + val2 > max_span_log_prob[b]:
+                # This is technically an incorrect implementation of max_span_length constraints
+                if val1 + val2 > max_span_log_prob[b] and j - span_start_argmax[b] < max_span_length:
                     best_word_span[b, 0] = span_start_argmax[b]
                     best_word_span[b, 1] = j
                     max_span_log_prob[b] = val1 + val2
```

This is not quite correct, because you really need to update `span_start_argmax[b]` and `val1` before these lines for this to really be correct.

It should be possible to do this with an outer product, and finding a diagonal block in that outer product, then taking the argmax.

issue_title: Use DSC replicate number directly as seed for RNG
issue_body: In the current implementation, the default seed for RNG is `DSC_REPLICATE_ID + SCRIPT_ID` where `SCRIPT_ID` is the current `md5sum` hash of the script file, converted to long-int. But I'm a bit bothered by the fact that each time I change the script, the results change, because the seed changes ... I think we should just use `DSC_REPLICATE_ID` as the default. If users need fancier behavior they should just set their own seeds.

issue_title: Content handling error
issue_body:
Hello,

There's a couple of changes that I've noted are needed for Python 3.5 support, specifically in relation to how meta-classes are handled, as well as the various print() requirements.  In addition to this, a couple constants need to be made into binary fields, not huge stuff.

The main issue I'm running into is that the current version here doesn't support the new 'entryMode' field on the transactionResponse.  Having enabled the production XSD and rebuilding it, it appears that pyxb or the class is breaking on an error with accountType.

I also noted that this system swallows exceptions, leading to situations where our systems were finishing a transaction and not aborting, as our try-excepts were not tripping thanks to the logging being sent to logging.debug, rather than sending up an exception.

Any chance these could be looked into?  My branch is fairly hacked up, and likely will break py 2.x support.

Thanks!
Alexander Blair

issue_title: Application healthcheck endpoint for checks during deploy and publishing
issue_body:
During an application release/deploy, the container just checks to make sure that the fleet unit reports that it is running: https://github.com/deis/deis/blob/c2cffb969a1edaaa456033a05b7a6958eea02bad/controller/scheduler/fleet.py#L193

Just because a unit is running doesn't mean that it's ready/healthy.

Additionally, there is actually a bug in the way that publisher checks for applications to be listening before it publishes them - @gabrtv discovered that the Docker proxy responsible for routing requests from the host into containers will actually accept on the external port immediately (even if no daemon in the container is bound to that port). 

The proposal is to:
- Add a new `healthcheck` endpoint and CLI command to configure a healthcheck endpoint for the application
- Modify the controller so that during a deploy, controller will wait for each deployed container to report healthy if a healthcheck endpoint is configured for the application
- Modify the publisher to check for an appropriate (HTTP 200?) response from the healthcheck endpoint before publishing an app container if a healthcheck endpoint is configured for the application

This solution addresses several issues and feature requests surrounding application deploys and health checks in the router, including #3478, #3723, and #3674.

issue_title: FreeTDS: cannot execute with params
issue_body:
Using FreeTDS I cannot execute an INSERT statement with parameters. I get the following error:
```
DatabaseError: ODBC error
state: IM001
native error code: 0
message: [unixODBC][Driver Manager]Driver does not support this function
```

From the unixODBC trace:
```
[ODBC][58773][1487672064.768219][SQLDescribeParam.c][185]
		Entry:
			Statement = 0x7fee40014400
			Parameter Number = 1
			SQL Type = 0x7fff5e9343be
			Param Def = 0x7fff5e9343b0
			Scale = 0x7fff5e9343ae
			Nullable = 0x7fff5e9343ac
[ODBC][58773][1487672064.768731][SQLDescribeParam.c][292]Error: IM001
```

To reproduce:
```python
conn = turbodbc.connect(dsn='mssql', uid='test', pwd='test')
cursor = conn.cursor()
cursor.execute("CREATE TABLE test_insert (val INT);")
cursor.execute("INSERT INTO test_insert (val) VALUES (?);", (0,))
```

Doing an insert without parameterization works fine, e.g.
`cursor.execute("INSERT INTO test_insert (val) VALUES (42);")`
works fine.

Full specs: using FreeTDS 1.00.26, unixODBC 2.3.4, Python 3.5.2, macOS Sierra, SQL Server 2016.

issue_title: Use separate data directories for light/full sync
issue_body:
### What is wrong?

Trinity currently doesn't use different data directories for `full` and `light` mode. It should as toggling between these modes will yield unexpected results with a shared database

### How can it be fixed

It's probably best to just use a `full` / `light` suffix depending on the passed mode *except* if the `data_dir` is passed explicitly as a command line option in which case we should just trust the user that they know what they are doing.

issue_title: Better codec for relations files
issue_body:
Relations files are yet another micro-format in use in DELPH-IN software, but PyDelphin only reads them. The ability to write them would also be useful at times. Also, the ability to read/write them from/to strings rather than files would be nice. E.g.

```python
relations = itsdb.Relations('''
item:
  i-id :integer :key
  ...
''')
relations.write(filename)
```

issue_title: Error while trying to parse a version number cascaded
issue_body:
**I'm submitting a...** - [x] bug report - [ ] feature request ## Current Behavior It seems that one of the conda-forge recipes upstream has a very weird version number. I expect conda to fall over trying to parse the version (I have a `except Exception as e` clause to prevent us from falling over in these situations). What I didn't expect was `__str__` in the top level init to fall over. ### Steps to Reproduce Wait an hour and watch the bot fall over again? :smile: (I'll try to do a better job figuring out what the upstream is) ``` class: CondaValueError message: Malformed version string 'CMIP6_CVs.6.2.8.23%2FDREQ.01.00.24%2FCMOR.3.3.3': invalid character(s). kwargs: {} class: CondaValueError message: Malformed version string 'CMIP6_CVs.6.2.8.23%2FDREQ.01.00.24%2FCMOR.3.3.3': invalid character(s). kwargs: {} concurrent.futures.process._RemoteTraceback: """ Traceback (most recent call last): File "/opt/conda/lib/python3.6/concurrent/futures/process.py", line 175, in _process_worker r = call_item.fn(*call_item.args, **call_item.kwargs) File "/root/repo/cf-scripts/conda_forge_tick/update_upstream_versions.py", line 195, in get_latest_version ver = source.get_version(url) File "/root/repo/cf-scripts/conda_forge_tick/update_upstream_versions.py", line 67, in get_version return max(vers, key=lambda x: VersionOrder(x.replace("-", "."))) File "/opt/conda/lib/python3.6/site-packages/conda/models/version.py", line 163, in __new__ raise CondaValueError(message + "invalid character(s).") conda.exceptions.CondaValueError: """ The above exception was the direct cause of the following exception: Traceback (most recent call last): File "/root/repo/cf-scripts/conda_forge_tick/update_upstream_versions.py", line 218, in update_upstream_versions attrs['new_version'] = f.result() File "/opt/conda/lib/python3.6/concurrent/futures/_base.py", line 425, in result return self.__get_result() File "/opt/conda/lib/python3.6/concurrent/futures/_base.py", line 384, in __get_result raise self._exception conda.exceptions.CondaValueErrorclass: CondaValueError message: Malformed version string 'CMIP6_CVs.6.2.8.23%2FDREQ.01.00.24%2FCMOR.3.3.3': invalid character(s). kwargs: {} : During handling of the above exception, another exception occurred: Traceback (most recent call last): File "/opt/conda/bin/conda-forge-tick", line 7, in exec(compile(f.read(), __file__, 'exec')) File "/root/repo/cf-scripts/scripts/conda-forge-tick", line 3, in main() File "/root/repo/cf-scripts/conda_forge_tick/cli.xsh", line 21, in main int_script_dict[script]() File "/root/repo/cf-scripts/conda_forge_tick/update_upstream_versions.py", line 252, in main update_upstream_versions(gx) File "/root/repo/cf-scripts/conda_forge_tick/update_upstream_versions.py", line 221, in update_upstream_versions "Error getting uptream version of {}: {}".format(node, e)) File "/opt/conda/lib/python3.6/site-packages/conda/__init__.py", line 48, in __str__ return text_type(self.message % self._kwargs) TypeError: must be real number, not dict ``` ## Expected Behavior Ideally conda wouldn't fall over twice when confrunted with an odd upstream version. ## Environment Information I'm working on these (I'm waiting on circle to try this again).

issue_title: conda activate/deactivate ruins PS1 in 4.3.14
issue_body:
In 4.3.1, source activate calls "deactivate for previous envs... line 62:

```
 60 # Ensure we deactivate any scripts from the old env
 61 _CONDA_HOLD=true
 62 source "$_CONDA_DIR/deactivate"
 63 unset _CONDA_HOLD
```

But if this is no previous environment ($CONDA_PS1_BACKUP is unset), PS1 is set to null and therefore a subsequent deactivate cannot retrieve the original PS1
[The logic around _CONDA_HOLD is broken in deactivate]

```
foobar:~ ds$ echo $PS1
\h:\W \u\$
foobar:~ ds$ echo $CONDA_PS1_BACKUP

foobar:~ ds$ source activate magenta
(magenta) echo $PS1
(magenta)
(magenta) echo $CONDA_PS1_BACKUP

(magenta) source deactivate

date
Wed Mar 22 08:03:17 PDT 2017
```
--> Lost prompt

issue_title: blosc-aware packages not getting full performance
issue_body:
I have detected an important lack of performance on blosc-aware packages.  For example, for bcolz in Anaconda, I am getting this:

```
In [1]: import numpy as np

In [2]: import bcolz

In [3]: a = np.arange(1e8)

In [4]: %timeit ca = bcolz.carray(a, cparams=bcolz.cparams(cname='lz4'))
10 loops, best of 3: 174 ms per loop

In [5]: bcolz.print_versions()
-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=
bcolz version:     1.0.0
NumPy version:     1.11.1
Blosc version:     1.8.1 ($Date:: 2016-04-08 #$)
Blosc compressors: ['blosclz', 'lz4', 'lz4hc', 'snappy', 'zlib']
Numexpr version:   2.6.1.dev0
Python version:    3.5.2 |Continuum Analytics, Inc.| (default, Jul  2 2016, 17:53:06) 
[GCC 4.4.7 20120313 (Red Hat 4.4.7-1)]
Platform:          linux-x86_64
Byte-ordering:     little
Detected cores:    8
-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=
```

Whereas in a local compiled version (Linux/gcc 5.4.0), I am getting this:

```
In [1]: import numpy as np

In [2]: import bcolz

In [3]: a = np.arange(1e8)

In [4]: %timeit ca = bcolz.carray(a, cparams=bcolz.cparams(cname='lz4'))
10 loops, best of 3: 104 ms per loop

In [5]: bcolz.print_versions()
-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=
bcolz version:     1.0.0
bcolz git info:    b'1.0.0'
NumPy version:     1.11.1
Blosc version:     1.8.1 ($Date:: 2016-04-08 #$)
Blosc compressors: ['blosclz', 'lz4', 'lz4hc', 'snappy', 'zlib']
Numexpr version:   2.6.1.dev0
Python version:    3.5.2 |Continuum Analytics, Inc.| (default, Jul  2 2016, 17:53:06) 
[GCC 4.4.7 20120313 (Red Hat 4.4.7-1)]
Platform:          linux-x86_64
Byte-ordering:     little
Detected cores:    8
-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=
```

which is a 1.7x faster than the version in Anaconda.

My hunch is that Anaconda is compiled (for compatibility reasons) with a relatively old compiler (judging by the logs above gcc 4.4.7?), which looses a lot of the advantages that optimizers in newer compilers are offering.  I am wondering if this could be solved somehow?  Thanks!

issue_title: conda installed package lacks installed files metadata required by pip and confuses users
issue_body:
`pip show --files numpy` fails to return the list of installed files of this package when it was installed with conda. The list of installed files of a python package is expected to be found in the `site-packages/package_name-version.egg-info/installed-files.txt` entry of packages installed by setuptools or better in the [officially documented](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0376/#one-dist-info-directory-per-installed-distribution) `packages/package_name-version.dist-info/RECORD`, for instance when a package is [installed by pip from wheel](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0427/#the-dist-info-directory).

This is problematic because this prevents pip from correctly uninstalling or upgrading a package previously installed by conda.

I know that packages installed by conda are supposed to be upgraded or removed by conda instead of pip but many users are not aware of that and the resulting error messages caused by a partially upgraded packages are very confusing, see for instance the discussion: https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/issues/4472.

issue_title: Conda should handle anaconda-server channels better
issue_body:
@ilanschnell @asmeurer It would be great to clean up the user experience when accessing anaconda-server with conda.

 1. Add conda user-agent like Binstar/binstar#138 so binstar can direct the user to the correct URL. (#801)
 1. Add a check (#1189) so that issues like Binstar/binstar#138 won't look like conda errors.
 1. Add a `.condarc` flag like "check_anaconda_server_login: [true|false]"  That will always ensure that the user is logged into anaconda-server before attempting to fetch the repodata.
    If `check_anaconda_server_login` is true then if a user is not logged in, a warning should be printed
    
    ```
    [Warning] you are not logged into anaconda-server. You may not be able to install private packages.
    You can log in with the command `binstar login` If you don't have an account you can sign up at <anaconda-server-url>
    ```
 
 1. Deprecate or warn on the the explicit use of urls with `/t/TOKEN` logging in should be the preferred way to access private packages.

issue_title: Problem: Downloading source fails for some packages
issue_body:
I get a Download error on downloading a certain package (patchelf). Its strange, because others work. I'm on latest master 4.3.2+34

  ```
[..] python3.5/site-packages/conda/gateways/download.py", line 99, in download
    downloaded_bytes=streamed_bytes)
conda.CondaError: Downloaded bytes did not match Content-Length
  url: http://nixos.org/releases/patchelf/patchelf-0.9/patchelf-0.9.tar.gz
  target_path: /[..]/conda-bld/src_cache/patchelf-0.9.tar.gz
  Content-Length: 191502
  downloaded bytes: 768000
```

The downloaded file in the src_cache is ok, md5sums match. Running conda build a second time works because it takes it from the src_cache. So I guess its a problem in the downloader. I could reproduce this error with this file only by now. Changing tar.gz to tar.bz2 (which is also offered by this vendor) makes it work. Other packages with tar.bz2 and tar.gz work fine though.

The number looks strange, its too perfect.. maybe an overflow or smthg?

In any case: Maybe its useful to clean up the src_cache after error (guess thats a conda-build issue then), because if the download would be corrupted for real, then we'd end up with a corrupted file that gets picked up next time...

issue_title: cannot drop view whily syncing
issue_body:
Hi,
we saw that there is an issues with syncing views, in case you change views that are dependencies for other views:

#### How to reproduce the error
1) create 2 Views like this
```
class Test1View(django_pgviews.View):
    sql = "SELECT last_name, email from auth_user;"

    class Meta:
        db_table = 'myapp_test1view'
        managed = False


class Test2View(django_pgviews.View):
    dependencies = [
        'myapp.Test1View',
    ]
    sql = "SELECT email from common_test1view;"

    class Meta:
        db_table = 'common_test2view'
        managed = False
```
2) run `manage.py sync_pgviews --force`

3) change the sql of `Test1View` to be `SELECT last_name, first_name, email from auth_user;`

4) run `manage.py sync_pgviews --force`
--> You will encounter the following error during sync:
`psycopg2.InternalError: cannot drop view myapp_test1view because other objects depend on it`



I will try to come up with a fix for this, unless you know another way how to get around this error.

issue_title: ForeignKey in view?
issue_body:
Hi,

Maybe this is a dumb question, but is it possible to define a pg.View with a ForeignKey in its projection? The reason I ask, is its natural given an model with an IP address in a model (and postgres) to say something like:

select v.*,o.id AS organisation_id from data_visitor v LEFT OUTER JOIN v.source_ip <<= data_network n LEFT OUTER JOIN data_organisation o ON n.organisation_id = o.id
   
and then say:
class VisitorOrganisation(pg.View):
    projection = ['Visitor.*', models.ForeignKey(...)]
    sql = ...

but this fails for me under django v1.8 - models.IntegerField in place of the foreign key is an ugly hack, but it works. Perhaps there is a better way to achieve the ability to identify an organisation for a given visitor's IP address using the postgres specific SQL?

issue_title: [Generic Update] Should be able to index into a collection by key
issue_body:
Related to item #686. Currently the only way to index into a collection is by index. In reality, most (not all) of the items in these collections have some unique key. We should support a syntax similar to:

`myobject.mylist['key'='value']....`

Here key is the name of the key property (name, id, etc.). Numeric indexing only works if the collection is stable, which is an implementation detail we should not rely upon.

Command

Arguments
    --alert-rule-resource-name [Required]: The name of the alert rule.
    --condition                [Required]: JSON encoded condition configuration. Use @{file} to load
                                           from a file.
    --is-enabled               [Required]: The flag that indicates whether the alert rule is
                                           enabled.

                                           alert rule becomes active, and when an alert condition is

                                           in the alert email.

                                           `az configure --defaults location=<location>`.  Default:

                                           group using `az configure --defaults group=<name>`.

issue_title: [Network] AppGateway frontend-ip subnet cannot be different from gateway subnet
issue_body:
When trying to test the `application-gateway frontend-ip update` command to change the subnet for an IP configuration, I got the following error:

```
FrontendIpConfiguration Microsoft.Network/applicationGateways/ag/frontendIPConfigurations/myfrontend subnet Microsoft.Network/virtualNetworks/vnet2/subnets/subnet1 and gateway subnet Microsoft.Network/virtualNetworks/vnet1/subnets/subnet1 can not be different.
```

This being the case `application-gateway frontend-ip update`: `--vnet-name`, `--subnet` parameters are useless.

There are a couple options:
1. Remove `--vnet-name` and `--subnet` parameters entirely, so that you can only update the static private IP address
2. Ensure that changes to the IP config subnet are propagated to the gateway IP config and vice versa (NOTE: there's no way to change the gateway ip config currently except through generic update).

issue_title: Storage queue service should automatically encode/decode base 64
issue_body:
### Description:
The other SDKs (.NET, Node.js) do that, so if you want to interop with them, it's currently the user's responsibility to do the encoding/decoding when using the Python SDK.

We should make it transparent and automatically encode/decode base64 when sending/receiving queue messages.

This would be a breaking change.

If we don't do this, we should fix the docs at http://azure.microsoft.com/en-us/documentation/articles/storage-python-how-to-use-queue-storage to show the encoding/decoding code required on the client's side.

##### Original Issue: https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python/issues/150
### Comments (2):
#### @cheif commented on 2014-04-25 15:01:51:
> I totally agree, the main idea of a SDK is to abstract away the implementation-details of the underlying resource, such as the transport using base64.

I think the best way to break the API is to bundle the breaking changes into a new major release (that would be 1.0.0 in this case), I think that there are some other things that also could be have a better abstraction (e.g. that StorageQueue.get_messages() should be a generator an not take a num_messages argument, that is also a leaky abstraction) or could be more pythonic (e.g. that QueueMessage-objects should be able to delete themselves, without using a StorageQueue-object, and a Blob-object being able to read their own data)

EDIT:
I realize that the "more-pythonic" changes can be implemented without breaking the API, I'd be happy to create a PR if others also think it's a good idea?

#### @saschagottfried commented on 2014-05-28 09:22:53:
> I had this issue this week with version 0.8.0 while developing code that receives queue messages transporting just a GUID that was inserted by .NET code. Since the .NET client did not insert XML or JSON into queue message I did not recognize the data as a base64 string. After checking .NET code is using usual code to insert queues messages, I eventually found MSDN information regarding base64 encoding/decoding of queue message. 

I expected this topic to be in discussion, so I am happy that package developers themselves make that an issue.

base64 decoding was not a big deal. The real problem at least for me was that I, as a developer, was not aware of that need to decode. Help developer to be aware that there are conditions where message.message_text is a base64 encoded string and therefore has to be decoded. And yes, update your docs as a first step towards a more pythonic Message Queue API. Otherwise you may risk to reduce Python developer satisfaction. That's not what you want since Azure rocks.

@cheif 
just do it and make a pull request. Open source is meant for contributions.

issue_title: Intermixing Mongo API and DocumentDB API breaks document structure
issue_body:
Working with Cosmos I initially loaded documents through the Mongo API. When shifting over to using pydocumentdb I've run into formatting issues with the generated results ($t and $v key-values are inserted throughout the document).

issue_title: Points generated by angular perturbation are wrong when object A is rotated
issue_body:
The following gist demonstrates that the location of contact points depends on the order of calls to `btCollisionWorld::addCollisionObject`.

https://gist.github.com/radarsat1/2eb69623b46ee10dfdb52ac617bcc658

It can be run with,

    ./collision_points_object_order | python - &

The program just sets up a collision world with a smaller box as a btConvexHullShape sitting on top of a larger box as a btBoxShape.
With current master (e87060dcf4b445c5c041933b60b9deff9ef98568) it produces the following output:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/66805/36311249-73a25246-1309-11e8-8805-8d2de7f52004.png)

Here, points on object A (i.e. getBody0) are blue, points on object B (getBody1) are red.  It seems that btConvexConvexAlgorithm correctly always selects to perturb the smaller object (the convex hull) but when it is A it generates the wrong points.  That is, they are not on the surface of either object.  I think the same points should be generated in both cases.  I think it is a wrong direction of the normal, or a wrong calculation.  I found a possible solution but am still verifying it.  I will submit a PR soon.

issue_title: Contact point persists when objects are far apart.
issue_body:
I have a simple scene containing two boxes. I am looking at the contact points between them and I frequently see one lingering contact point despite the objects not being in contact.

![contact](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/120369/41111949-6794ef22-6a75-11e8-8067-e74f5269924a.gif)

I have been able to track this down to how contact points are reused. Initially, as the first box just begins to intersect the second, all contact points have normals pointing to the left since this is the direction of least penetration (all as expected).

Then, at a later point, the direction of least penetration changes so that all the normals should be pointing upwards. At this point, the insertion index for two of the contact points determined inside of `btManifoldResult::addContactPoint()` is -1 because the minimum distance is greater than the contact breaking threshold. All fine so far.

For those two contact points we call `btPersistentManifold::addManifoldPoint()`, which obtains the insertion index from `btPersistentManifold::sortCachedPoints()`. This function retains the contact point with the greatest penetration depth. And herein lies the problem...

We retain one of the two contact points with the normals pointing to the left. This will from now on always have the greatest penetration distance, which will continue to grow in magnitude. The `btPersistentManifold::refreshContactPoints()` function will then never remove the contact point. The contact point only goes away when the broadphase determines that the two boxes are definitely not intersecting.

issue_title: web.FileResponse overrides content_type
issue_body:
## Long story short

When returning a `web.FileResponse` from a route, any changes to the `content_type` member is not taken into account when the file is served. Instead, the file extension is always used to determine the content type of the response.

## Expected behaviour

`Content-Type` header of the response be that of `web.FileResponse.content_type` when such has been set.

## Actual behaviour

`Content-Type` header is guessed from the file extension of the served file.

## Steps to reproduce

Create a route that responds something along the lines of

```python
    response = web.FileResponse('/path/to/some/image.png')
    response.content_type = 'application/octet-stream'
    return response
```

then `curl -i` it and notice the `Content-Type` is `image/png`.

## Your environment

```
[~]$ uname -mrs
Linux 4.11.9-1-ARCH x86_64
[~]$ python --version
Python 3.6.2
[~]$ pip show aiohttp | grep Version
Version: 2.2.5
```

issue_title: unknown device error on pushtotalk
issue_body:
The pushtotalk.py shows the following error on a device with a trait action.devices.traits.OnOff:

```
Press Enter to send a new request...
INFO:root:Recording audio request.
...
INFO:root:Transcript of user request: "turn on".
INFO:root:Playing assistant response.
WARNING:root:Ignoring command for unknown device: xxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxxx
INFO:root:Finished playing assistant response.

```

The error indicates that a [DeviceRequestHandler](https://github.com/googlesamples/assistant-sdk-python/blob/master/google-assistant-sdk/googlesamples/assistant/grpc/device_helpers.py#L76) was not initialised with a proper device_id.

In [pushtotalk.py](https://github.com/googlesamples/assistant-sdk-python/blob/master/google-assistant-sdk/googlesamples/assistant/grpc/pushtotalk.py#L358), the DeviceRequestHandler is initialised before loading device_config, but it should be done after loading the device_config so that it can read proper device_id from the device_config.

issue_title: nbgrader cell grading issues: kernel not agnostic, confusing, English
issue_body:
Currently, when you create a nbgrader notebook and mark a cell to be autograded, it will replace the cell's contents (if any) with:

```python
# YOUR CODE HERE
raise NotImplementedError()
```

This is confusing for two reasons:

1) If you actually put in code, such as:

```scheme
(define my-test-function
    (lambda (arg)
        ;; your code here...
```
it is just removed.

2) It is replaced with Python code. Beginning Python students are confused by these lines. It is very confusing if they are beginning students of a different language.

Constraints: there is an automatic feature that will replace/search for text, such as :

```
### BEGIN SOLUTION
...
### END SOLUTION
```
This is, of course, language specific (eg, the comment hash is specific to Python). 

A related problem is that text Markdown cells that are marked for autograding are also removed. For example, the text is always replaced with:

```
YOUR ANSWER HERE
```

My overall feeling is that nbgrader is attempting to do too much, and that causes issues because what it does do is not kernel (nor human-language) agnostic. If it instead left the text/code alone the cells, many of these issues would go away.

But what to do about automatic solution hiding? Perhaps something more general:

* if a line contains the text "*** begin solution ***" (can be embedded in contents of kernel), then start of solution
* if a line contains the text "*** end solution ***" (can be embedded in contents of kernel), then end of solution

Also, the system should not rely on raising NotImplementedError(). This is confusing to see, and is a weak way to check for "validation".

issue_title: Migrate to pytest
Fields order is not preserved during validation
issue_body:
The docs state: "The order whith which fields are declared is preserved inside the model." [Link](https://schematics.readthedocs.org/en/latest/usage/validation.html?highlight=order%20preserved#model-level-validation). However, it's not actually true... 

Model stores its fields as an OrderedDict, so the order is fixed / preserved. However, validation process is a loop over [_data_ to be validated](https://github.com/j2labs/schematics/blob/master/schematics/validate.py#L76), not fields set. Data comes to validate() function from [import_loop()](https://github.com/j2labs/schematics/blob/master/schematics/validate.py#L41) that for its turn considers data as a regular [dict](https://github.com/j2labs/schematics/blob/master/schematics/transforms.py#L52)


Example below shows that validate_call_me() method gets hit _before_ validate_name() although the fields are defined in reversed order.

```
from schematics.models import Model
from schematics.types import StringType, BooleanType
from schematics.exceptions import ValidationError

class Signup(Model):
    name = StringType()
    call_me = BooleanType(default=False)
    
    def validate_name(self, data, value):
        if data['name'] == u'Brad':
            return u'Joe'
        return value
    
    def validate_call_me(self, data, value):
        print 'validate_call_me: {}'.format(data['name'])
        if data['name'] == u'Joe' and data['call_me'] is True:
            raise ValidationError(u'He prefers email.')
        return value
## -- End pasted text --

In [32]: Signup({'name': u'Brad', 'call_me': True}).validate()
validate_call_me: Brad
```

Please, let me know if it makes sense.

issue_title: Sign flipped in stats.multivariate_normal
issue_body:
G'day developers

I love autograd, thanks for the hard work.

It is probably my own error, but I suspect a flipped sign in the derivative of the multivariate normal log density wrt its covariance. Here's a minimal example, or see [this Colab notebook](https://colab.research.google.com/drive/1F5Y1sQzVZT18p7qBGsTUYPXIuYiRy4NK):

```
from autograd import grad
from autograd import numpy as np
from autograd.scipy import stats

# some constants
s = np.array([[2.]]); mu = 2.; x = 1.

# two log density functions that should be the same (univariate and 1x1 multivariate)
def llik(sigma):
  return stats.norm.logpdf(x, np.array(mu), sigma)
def llik_mv(sigma):
  vcv = np.dot(sigma, sigma.T)  # square to get a 1x1 covariance matrix
  return stats.multivariate_normal.logpdf(np.array([[x]]), [mu], vcv)

print('Gradient of log pdf wrt standard deviation:')
print('autograd gradient: norm %.3f  multivariate_normal %.3f' % (
    grad(llik)(s), grad(llik_mv)(s)))

# manually differentiate numerically as a sense check
eps = 1e-7
print('numeric check:     norm %.3f  multivariate_normal %.3f' % (
    (llik(s+eps) - llik(s))/eps, (llik_mv(s+eps) - llik_mv(s))/eps))
```

And the output, on python 3.6.3:
```
Gradient of log pdf wrt standard deviation:
autograd gradient: norm -0.375  multivariate_normal 0.375
numeric check:     norm -0.375  multivariate_normal -0.375
```

I expected both autograd gradients to match, as they do in the numerical version. Have I messed up, or is something wrong here? 

Thanks again for a wonderful library
Alex

issue_title: TODO: Update existing checkout of scikit-build
issue_body:
Et voila. 

As discussed in https://github.com/scikit-build/scikit-build/issues/85#issuecomment-234397635, "Reorganized master" has been forced pushed:

* History is now coherent
* Each change introduce only one set of change with a descriptive commit message

Based on your current situation, consider following the instructions reported below:

Cc: @scikit-build/developers 

### Existing checkout: without topic

```
git fetch origin
git reset --hard origin/master
```

If you also have a reference to your fork on github, consider also updating it:

```
git push name_of_your_fork master --force
```


### Existing checkout:  with topic - rebase instructions

To rebase your topic named `name_of_your_topic`:

```
git checkout master && git branch -M master-original
git fetch origin
git checkout -b master origin/master
git checkout name_of_your_topic
git rebase --onto master master-original name_of_your_topic
```

### More details

There are no differences between the reorganized master (be4a55e) and the former one (169894b):

```

issue_title: Submitting information to Libre.fm not possible
issue_body:
I want to scrobble to Libre.fm and update the now playing status.

```python
network = LibreFMNetwork(username='my_user_name',
                         password_hash=md5('my_password'))
```
This does not throw an error.

Using `network.update_now_playing()` or `network.scrobble()` with the same parameters I successfully use for Last.fm-scrobbling, I will get the following error:
```python
WARNING  Error submitting playing track to Libre.fm: {'status': u'6', 'details': u'Invalid parameters - Your request is missing a required parameter', 'network': pylast.LibreFMNetwork('', '', '', 'my_user_name', 'my_password_hash')}
```

Inspecting the `network` object, I see that it is missing a session key (the Last.fm network is not), which to my knowledge should actually be automatically generated given `username` and `password_hash`.
```python
WARNING  {'username': u'my_user_name', 'cache_backend': None, 'session_key': '', 'name': 'Libre.fm', 'api_secret': '', 'domain_names': {0: 'alpha.libre.fm', 1: 'alpha.libre.fm', 2: 'alpha.libre.fm', 3: 'alpha.libre.fm', 4: 'alpha.libre.fm', 5: 'alpha.libre.fm', 6: 'alpha.libre.fm', 7: 'alpha.libre.fm', 8: 'alpha.libre.fm', 9: 'alpha.libre.fm', 10: 'alpha.libre.fm', 11: 'alpha.libre.fm'}, 'proxy_enabled': False, 'submission_server': 'http://turtle.libre.fm:80/', 'proxy': None, 'urls': {'album': 'artist/%(artist)s/album/%(album)s', 'playlist': 'user/%(user)s/library/playlists/%(appendix)s', 'tag': 'tag/%(name)s', 'group': 'group/%(name)s', 'user': 'user/%(name)s', 'artist': 'artist/%(artist)s', 'track': 'music/%(artist)s/_/%(title)s', 'country': 'place/%(country_name)s', 'event': 'event/%(id)s'}, 'last_call_time': 0, 'limit_rate': False, 'api_key': '', 'homepage': 'http://alpha.libre.fm', 'ws_server': ('alpha.libre.fm', '/2.0/'), 'password_hash': 'my_password_hash'}
```

Manually retrieving the session key fails.

```python
# my code:
SessionKeyGenerator(network.get_session_key(network.username, network.password_hash))

# the error:
ERROR    Triggering event failed: track_playback_started
Traceback (most recent call last):
[...]
  File "/usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/pylast/__init__.py", line 1258, in get_session_key
    doc = request.execute()
  File "/usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/pylast/__init__.py", line 1137, in execute
    response = self._download_response()

    self._check_response_for_errors(response_text)

    raise WSError(self.network, status, details)
WSError: Invalid authentication token supplied
```

I see this behaviour when using pylast 1.4.2 as well as 1.5.1. Again, I want to stress that everything works perfectly fine with Last.fm. If it matters, my password is quite long (>60 chars) and contains several special characters.

issue_title: BatchReshape and BatchFlatten don't work in the straight-forward way
issue_body:
Dear engineers, I just want to convert two 4x4 gray images (now the shape is (2, 4, 4, 1)) into some layout suitable for layer normalization ( the intended shape is (2, 16), let's assume here I am sane to do so) and then convert it back. The straight forward way is to utilize BatchFlatten and BatachReshape in pair to do the job. But such a natural idea seems to be not workable here:

```
import tensorflow as tf
import sonnet as snt

class MyLN(snt.AbstractModule):
    def __init__(self, name = "my_ln"):
        super(MyLN, self).__init__(name = name)
        with self._enter_variable_scope():
            self._ln = snt.LayerNorm()

    def _build(self, inputs):
        return snt.BatchReshape(inputs.get_shape().as_list())(self._ln(snt.BatchFlatten()(inputs)))

t = tf.truncated_normal([2, 4, 4, 1])

n = MyLN()(t)

with tf.Session() as sess:
    sess.run(tf.global_variables_initializer())

    t_val, n_val = sess.run([t, n])

    print(t_val)
    print(n_val)

```

I just wonder have I misunderstood something about these two modules? Thanks a lot for pointing out that.

issue_title: Troubles with setuptools_scm
issue_body:
Hello.

Because pytest-faulthandler uses setuptools_scm to determine package version, it's impossible to build pytest-faulthandler from source tarball downloaded from Github or PyPI.

All tries end with this error:
```
LookupError: setuptools-scm was unable to detect version for '/home/lbalhar/Packages/python-pytest-faulthandler/pytest-faulthandler-1.5.0'.
```
If I understand how setuptools_scm works, it needs `.git` folder or `PKG-INFO` file to determine package version but tarballs from Github and PyPI don't contain these metadata.
It's obvious why `.git` folder isn't part of mentioned tarballs but it's strange why PyPI tarball doesn't contain `PKG-INFO` file when it's created automatically by `python setup.py sdist` (tested). Are you using this command to create a source tarball for PyPI?

I don't know if it's a good idea to add `PKG-INFO` file directly to this project but pytest, for example, has one. Probably simplest solution here is to recreate PyPI tarball with sdist command and check whether it contains `PKG-INFO` file.

What do you think about it?

```python
from f5.bigip import ManagementRoot
mgmt = ManagementRoot(hostname='10.10.1.202', port='80', username='myUsername', password='myPassword')
transaction = mgmt.tm.transactions.transaction
with TransactionContextManager(transaction, validate_only=False) as tx:

issue_title: Adding new *.cs file while having the property EnableDefaultCompileItems on false
issue_body:
# Creating new *.cs file while having the property EnableDefaultCompileItems on false does not automatically add this file in the project file

# General

- This is on Visual Studio 2017 Version 15.5.57
- Using the new SDK project format style
- Has `<EnableDefaultCompileItems>false</EnableDefaultCompileItems>`
- Has external files included using the `<Compile Include="" />` way
- Project was ported from the old .net framework project style to the new style as this project is now a netstandard2.0 project.

I can actually fix this issue very easily by just removing the <EnableDefaultCompileItems>false</EnableDefaultCompileItems> and removing the two files that are already included with the `<Compile Include="" />` 

However some solutions that we have, have cs files that are compiled with a Prebuild target. To use them while building, they needed  to be explicitly added in the project file and therefor the EnableDefaultCompileItems property was also used.

issue_title: Support multiple contact addresses
issue_body: The ACME protocol allows multiple contact addresses on a registration object. The client should allow specifying multiple addresses also. This is important if there are multiple people who need to be aware of certificate expiration events. See, for instance, https://community.letsencrypt.org/t/library-freedom-project-is-looking-for-people-who-can-help-libraries-with-https/1490.

issue_title: Store version string in logs
issue_body: It will help us in debugging, in particular `letsencrypt-auto` users that run versioned PyPI releases. That's probably good candidate for `logging.INFO` level.

issue_title: Clarify purpose of --email
issue_body:
This is another good issue pointed out by @centminmod. How is `--email` actually used? If you're automating running `letsencrypt`, should the flag be included every time? Does it need to be the same e-mail address each time? What happens if it's different?

The current behavior is `--email` is only used if you're creating a new account with the ACME server, otherwise the flag is ignored. Is this the behavior we want? Could we filter/choose your account based on the e-mail address if you have multiple ACME accounts? Even if we do not want to change its behavior, the purpose of this flag should be better documented.

Implementation code:

~~~
class StreamConsumer:
...

    def consume(self, topics, auto_offset='latest', processor=_printer, *args):
        """Connects to topic and listens for messages, handing them off to processor"""

        logger = self.__get_logger()

        try: 
            running = True
            
            conf = {
                'bootstrap.servers': ','.join(map(str, self.config.get('hosts'))),
                'group.id': self.group_id,
                'default.topic.config': {'auto.offset.reset': auto_offset}
            }

            try:
                c = kafka.Consumer(**conf)
            except:
                logger.error( "Error creating Consumer with config [{}]".format(conf) )

            try:
                if auto_offset == 'earliest' or auto_offset == 'smallest':
                    c.subscribe(topics, on_assign=_on_assign)
                else:
                    c.subscribe(topics)
            except:
                logger.error( "Error subscribing to topics [{}]".format(topics) )
                c = None

            if c:
                logger.info( "Starting to poll topics [{}]...".format(topics) )
                while running:
                    try:
                        msg = c.poll()
                        if not msg.error():
                            processor(msg)
                        elif msg.error().code() == kafka.KafkaError._PARTITION_EOF:
                            # End of partition event
                            logger.debug( "{} [{}] reached end at offset {}".format(
                                            msg.topic(), msg.partition(), msg.offset()) )
                        else:
                            logger.debug( "Unknown error [{}]. Quitting...".format(msg.error()) )
                            raise kafka.KafkaException(msg.error())
                    except:
                        running = False
                        logger.error( "Error polling messages." )
                # end while running loop
            else:
                logger.error( "Consumer object missing. Nothing to do!" )

            try:
                c.close() # close connection
            except:
                logger.warn( "Could not close connection (c). Nothing to do!" )

        except Exception, e:
            logger.error( "Error consuming topics [{}]".format(topics) )
            logger.debug( traceback.format_exc() )

        return None
~~~

I leveraged the suggested `_on_assign` syntax suggested in #11 (thanks again) if I decided to request `--from-beginning` upon startup.

~~~
def _on_assign (c, ps):
    """Resets the consumer offset"""
    for p in ps:
        p.offset=-2
    c.assign(ps)
~~~

Any ideas why it disconnected after an hour of inactivity after EOF? Thanks!

issue_title: Installing a pypiserver-hosted package from within a requirements.txt file fails, but doing so from the command line works.
issue_body:
First I'd like to say that I haven't been able to verify that this is *not* an issue with `pip` itself, so I'm sorry if it's on their end and not yours.

## Summary
Installing a `pypiserver`-hosted package from within a `requirements.txt` file fails, but doing so from the command line works.

## Versions
+ Python 3.5.2, 64-bit
+ Pip 9.0.1
+ pypiserver 1.2.0
+ OS: Windows 10 AU (running WinPython 3.5.2.2)

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Set up a `pypiserver` host and upload a package to it. For this example, we'll use a package called `gdw`
2. Create a clean venv locally and update `pip` to 9.0.1.
3. **Test Case 1:** From command line.
    + `python -m pip install gdw --index-url http://my_pypi.local/pypi`
    + Result: Complains about https, as expected.
    + `python -m pip install gdw --index-url http://my_pypi.local/pypi --trusted-host my_pypi.local`
    + Result: Success!
    + Remove the installed package: `python -m pip uninstall -y gdw`
4. **Test Case 2:** From requirements file.
    + Create a requirements.txt file with a single line: `gdw --index-url http://my_pypi.local/pypi`
    + `python -m pip install -r requirements.txt`
    + Result:
        ```
        Could not find a version that satisfies the requirement gdw (from -r requirements.txt (line 1)) (from versions: )
        No matching distribution found for gdw (from -r requirements.txt (line 1)
        ```
5. **Test Case 2b:** Maybe it doesn't like non-https and Pip is not raising that warning.
    + `python -m pip install -r requirements.txt --trusted-host my_pypi.local`
    + Result: Same thing
6. **Test Case 2c:** Well what if we put `--trusted-host` in the requirements file?
    + Change `requirements.txt` to `gdw --index-url http://my_pypi.local/pypi --trusted-host my_pypi.local`
    + `python -m pip install -r requirements.txt`
    + Result: same as before.

## Comments
According to pypa/pip#3007, it *should* accept `--trusted-host` inside of requirements files. However, comments in [pip#3799](https://github.com/pypa/pip/issues/3799#issuecomment-228840598) imply that it doesn't do the same thing as the command line option...

Here's an image of the PyPIServer index, so you can see that it has a version of `gdw` to pull (also the fact that Test Case 1 worked provides this info).
![image](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/5386897/23141782/4fff5df6-f76d-11e6-8d0d-09c2147efec8.png)

---

So, any thoughts on what this issue might be? Should I report this to the `pip` maintainers? I haven't been able to reproduce it without using `pypiserver` because I don't know of any other package indexes to use for `--index-url`.

Thanks,

issue_title: Issue with _flatten in v0.5 vs v0.3
issue_body:
Hi everyone,
  First, thanks for the great work on this library.  I noticed a small issue with the functioning of _flatten in release v0.5 compared to v0.3 having to do with the _flatten function in utilities.py.  In v0.3 I used a Pandas dataframe to feed a HeatMap object like so:

#### Please add a code sample or a nbviewer link, copy-pastable if possible

```python
import pandas as pd
import folium
from folium import plugins
pts = pd.DataFrame([[ 27.7336, -97.4218],
                    [ 41.2001, -90.7496],
                    [ 27.7522, -98.0733],
                    [ 42.0263, -93.6147],
                    [ 36.9755, -93.7223]])
m = folium.Map(location=[40.8716, -92.2727], zoom_start=4)
m.add_child(plugins.HeatMap(data=pts.values, radius = 15))
```
I get: `TypeError: only length-1 arrays can be converted to Python scalars`

This is due (I think) to the _flatten function in utilities.py only looking for lists or tuples, not arrays (as is output by Pandas `value` or `as_matrix` attribute).  This wasn't a problem in v0.3, but when I updated, it popped up as a new error.

Would it be helpful for me to fix this? Not sure if this change was by design or if I'm misunderstanding something about the error.

issue_title: apispec.utils.load_yaml_from_docstring should let YAMLError propagate
issue_body:
* I think the "---" prefix is enough to keep from accidentally parsing random docstrings as YAML
* the YAML parser raises very useful errors
* swallowing the YAML parser error silently is probably not *usually* doing the user a favor

```python
>>> docstring_with_no_yaml = """Some function docstring.
... 
... Nothing to see here.
... """
>>> docstring_with_yaml = """Some function docstring.
... ---
... get:
...   responses:
...     200:
...       schema: $ref: '#/definitions/Schema'
... """
>>> from apispec.utils import *
>>> load_yaml_from_docstring(docstring_with_no_yaml)
>>> # ok
... load_yaml_from_docstring(docstring_with_yaml)
>>> # huh?
... import yaml
>>> yaml.load(dedent('\n'.join(docstring_with_yaml.split('\n')[1:])))
Traceback (most recent call last):
[...]
yaml.scanner.ScannerError: mapping values are not allowed here
  in "<unicode string>", line 5, column 19:
          schema: $ref: '#/definitions/Schema'
                      ^ 
```

I propose changing

```python
def load_yaml_from_docstring(docstring):
    """Loads YAML from docstring."""
    split_lines = trim_docstring(docstring).split('\n')

    # Cut YAML from rest of docstring
    for index, line in enumerate(split_lines):
        line = line.strip()
        if line.startswith('---'):
            cut_from = index
            break
    else:
        return None

    yaml_string = "\n".join(split_lines[cut_from:])
    yaml_string = dedent(yaml_string)
    try:
        return yaml.load(yaml_string)
    except yaml.YAMLError:
        return None
```
to
```python
def load_yaml_from_docstring(docstring):
    """Loads YAML from docstring."""
    split_lines = trim_docstring(docstring).split('\n')

    # Cut YAML from rest of docstring
    for index, line in enumerate(split_lines):
        line = line.strip()
        if line.startswith('---'):
            cut_from = index
            break
    else:
        return None

    yaml_string = "\n".join(split_lines[cut_from:])
    yaml_string = dedent(yaml_string)
    return yaml.load(yaml_string)
```

issue_title: Field serialize default arguments
issue_body:
Currently [Field.serialize()](https://github.com/marshmallow-code/marshmallow/blob/dev/marshmallow/fields.py#L233) takes self and 2 arguments (attr and obj).

However in basic use cases, attr is not needed.

- [Nested](https://github.com/marshmallow-code/marshmallow/blob/dev/marshmallow/fields.py#L427)
- [List](https://github.com/marshmallow-code/marshmallow/blob/dev/marshmallow/fields.py#L547) -> depends on self.container
- [String](https://github.com/marshmallow-code/marshmallow/blob/dev/marshmallow/fields.py#L582)
- etc.

so I think the `attr` argument of `Field.serialize()` should be optional.

my current workaround is
```python
mybfield = Boolean()
wire_format = mybfield.serialize(0, [True])
assert mybfield.deserialize(wire_format)
```
but this would be nicer : 
```python
mybfield = Boolean()
wire_format = mybfield.serialize(True)
assert mybfield.deserialize(wire_format)
```

Also in term of API design this should make the `deserialize` function the exact inverse of `serialize` function.

issue_title: Inconsistent field names in error results.
issue_body:
I am using marshmallow 2.14.
Given following code:
```python
from marshmallow import Schema, fields, ValidationError, validates

class Broken(Schema):
    f = fields.String(required=True, load_from='f-name')
    f2 = fields.String(required=True, load_from='f2-name')

    @validates('f')
    def validate_string(self, data):
        raise ValidationError('nope')

_, err = Broken().load({'f': 'hello', 'f2': None}) # does not throw validation error
print(err)
```
I get following output:
```
{'f': ['nope'], 'f2-name': ['Field may not be null.']}
```
But I would expect to end up with:
```
{'f-name': ['nope'], 'f2-name': ['Field may not be null.']}
```
There is seems to be no way to override this behavior.
I suppose it should use load_from field on load and dump_to on dump for errors.

issue_title: Batch-dependent behavior in SoftmaxCEMaskedLoss?
issue_body:
Hi all,

Consider the following (placed in `scripts/nmt/`):
```python
import mxnet as mx
import loss

if __name__ == '__main__':
    BATCH_SIZE = 3
    MAX_SEQ_LEN = 100
    VOCAB_SIZE = 26
    # Each time step has a uniform distribution over the vocabulary
    uniform_tensor = 1./VOCAB_SIZE * mx.nd.ones(shape=(BATCH_SIZE, MAX_SEQ_LEN, VOCAB_SIZE))
    # Sequences have different lengths
    valid_lens = mx.nd.array((1, 10, 100))

    loss = loss.SoftmaxCEMaskedLoss(sparse_label=False, from_logits=False)
    ce_loss = loss(uniform_tensor, uniform_tensor, valid_lens)
    print(ce_loss)
```
This outputs:
```
[0.03258096 0.32580966 3.2580965 ]
<NDArray 3 @cpu(0)>
```
**However, these should all be `3.2580965` (which is ln(26))**, as this is the (average) CE across all valid timesteps, per sequence.

The problem is [the averaging step of CE is not aware of `valid_length`](https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/blob/master/python/mxnet/gluon/loss.py#L319), which leads to "masking zeros" being included in the mean. Compare with, e.g., [Sockeye's implementation](https://github.com/awslabs/sockeye/blob/master/sockeye/loss.py#L185).

One workaround is to increase sample weights so their weighted sum compensates for the zeros. For example, adding the following line before calling [super in SoftmaxCEMaskedLoss](https://github.com/dmlc/gluon-nlp/blob/master/scripts/nmt/loss.py#L56):
```
sample_weight = sample_weight * (sample_weight.shape[1] / F.reshape(valid_length, shape=(-1, 1, 1)))
```

I have not evaluated how this affects current MT models. This causes short sequences to have smaller per-token loss, weighting long sequences more.

However, perhaps we should edit `SoftmaxCEMaskedLoss` for correctness (or someone point out if I'm mistaken); I can submit a PR. The CE loss of an sequence should not depend on the batch it is in.

issue_title: Problem with generatiny explanations in Datalog engine
issue_body:
I have tried to coax some explanations out of the muZ datalog engine, but my attempts so far have been unsuccessful. To illustrate the issue, here is a short Python example adapted from the tutorial:

```
from z3 import *

fp = Fixedpoint()
fp.set(engine='datalog')
fp.set('datalog.generate_explanations', True)

a, b, c = Bools('a b c')

fp.register_relation(a.decl(), b.decl(), c.decl())
fp.rule(a,b, name='r1')
fp.rule(b,c, name='r2')
fp.fact(c)
print fp.query(a)
print fp.get_answer()
```

issue_title: model_eval returns identity on "don't cares"
issue_body:
Hi,

The C API documentation for `model_eval` specifies that setting `model_completion` to `false` would let the caller identify the identities.
I have (somehow naively) assumed that for don't-cares Z3 would not write anything into the given pointer, or write `null`.
Turns out it instead writes the same AST node it got as an input.
Is it a bug or a feature?
If latter, could it be documented?

issue_title: Addressing Context memory leakage in Java
issue_body: What is the status of Context cleanup in Java? Batfish (github/arifogel/batfish) uses JNI to spawn threads in parallel for an unlimited number of jobs (75 in my most recent use case), and it looks like these Contexts are not cleaning up after themselves after calling Context.dispose(). I even tried using -XX:+UseSerialGC since you seemed to imply it was necessary in issue #205 for soundness, but this doesn't seem to affect the garbage that never gets cleaned up when each job finishes. The process just ends up crashing after available memory is all allocated (or earlier if too much time is spent in GC). As such, the only way to run Batfish's Z3 analysis  in a single process for all these jobs is to have memory that scales with the total number of jobs, which is prohibitively expensive and unnecessary. I can try to rewrite my program to use multiple processes rather than threads so the memory is guaranteed to be freed, but that is a terrible hack and will require IPC I'd rather not implement.

issue_title: Optimization: incorrect answer =(
issue_body:
Hi,

This is somewhat new: I'm getting an answer which is actually incorrect for the optimization query.
The associated behavior is also very strange.
Here is my minimal reproducible example:

```
(declare-fun |main::i@3| () Int)
(declare-fun |main::i@4| () Int)
(declare-fun |main::source@2| () Int)
(declare-fun |main::nodecount@3| () Int)
(declare-fun |main::edgecount@3| () Int)

(assert
    (and 
       (and (= |main::i@4| (+ 1 |main::i@3|)))
       (<= |main::nodecount@3| 4)
       (<= (+ (* (- 1) |main::i@3|) |main::edgecount@3|) 19)
       (<= (* (- 1) |main::nodecount@3|) 0)
       (<= |main::edgecount@3| 19)
       (<= (* (- 1) |main::edgecount@3|) 0)
       (<= (+ |main::i@3| (* (- 1) |main::nodecount@3|)) 0)
       (<= (+ (* (- 1) |main::i@3|) |main::nodecount@3|) 4)
       (<= (+ (* (- 1) |main::i@3|) |main::source@2|) 0)
       (<= (* (- 1) |main::i@3|) 0)
       (<= (+ |main::i@3| (* (- 1) |main::source@2|)) 1)
       (<= |main::source@2| 0)
       (<= (* (- 1) |main::source@2|) 0)
       (<= |main::source@2| 0)
))
(maximize (* (- 1) |main::i@4|))
(set-option :opt.optsmt_engine basic)
(set-option :opt.priority box)
(check-sat)
(get-model)
```
Z3 gives me the answer "-18", and the model `i@3 = -19`.
Yet the constraint line clearly says: `(<= (* (- 1) |main::i@3|) 0)` (`i@3` has to be positive).

Moreover, the query exhibits many strange behaviors. For one, plugging in back model values as assertions expectedly gives "UNSAT" (by chance, would it be possible to have a "sanity-check-debug-mode" for optimization which would actually check that the obtained bound is feasible?)
Moreover, replacing the assertions `source@2 <= 0 /\ -source@2 <= 0` with `source@2 = 0` (which is the same), changes the answer to -1.

issue_body:
I built z3 with from the master branch commit [882c3bd](https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/commit/882c3bd0cdcfbaa0f8d0d7a04cee3009e151bb1e), and used it on the following input:

    ;; Command to run: 
    ;; z3 -smt2 smt.mbqi=true smt.macro-finder=true -T:20 bug.z3
    (declare-datatypes () ((Oper GB WD DP NOP)))
    (declare-datatypes () ((Eff e1 e2 e3 e4 e5)))
    (declare-fun oper (Eff) Oper)
    (declare-fun rval (Eff) Int)
    (assert (= (oper e3) GB))
    ;; e4 may be WD or NOP
    (assert (or (= (oper e4) WD) (= (oper e4) NOP)))
    ;; e5 may be WD or NOP
    (assert (or (= (oper e5) WD) (= (oper e5) NOP)))
    ;; assert soft constraints
    (declare-const b Bool)
    (assert-soft (not b))
    (check-sat) 
    (get-model)
    (eval (oper e1))
    (eval (oper e2))
    (eval (oper e3))
    (eval (oper e4))
    (eval (oper e5))

I get what appears to be an incorrect model:

```
$ z3 -smt2 smt.mbqi=true smt.macro-finder=true -T:20 bug.z3
sat
(objectives
 ( 0)
)
(model 
  (define-fun b () Bool
    false)
  (define-fun oper ((x!0 Eff)) Oper
    GB)
)
GB
GB
GB
GB
GB
```

Observe that `oper(e4)=GB` and `oper(e5)=GB` in the model, although hard constraints explicitly prohibit this. I also tried Z3 v4.4.1, but it throws a segmentation fault on the above input (my system is Mac OS X 10.9.5). Z3 behind the web interface seems to behave well, though.

#251 allowed us to specify the class used for temporary storage of a file uploaded in the admin by setting `tmp_storage_class` on the `ModelAdmin` (or other `ImportMixin` subclass).

i suggest here to add new command line switches to be able to set both ```mpv_version``` and ```mpv_options``` manually (by user). the reason would be that runnig ```mpv --version``` and especially ```mpv --list-options``` substantially contribute to the total startup time.

hardcoded ```mpv_version``` and ```mpv_options``` (removing function calls):

issue_title: `find_files_and_readers` doesn't work on Windows
issue_body:
As already briefly discussed at issue #366, parsing the reader yaml files causes the pattern to change the path separator depending on the platform. This results in the `satpy.find_files_and_readers` function to never return any results.

The path separator in the yaml files are, by convention, specified as `"/"`, for example the SLSTR yaml:

`'{mission_id:3s}_SL_{processing_level:1s}_{datatype_id:_<6s}_{start_time:%Y%m%dT%H%M%S}_{end_time:%Y%m%dT%H%M%S}_{creation_time:%Y%m%dT%H%M%S}_{duration:4d}_{cycle:3d}_{relative_orbit:3d}_{frame:4d}_{centre:3s}_{mode:1s}_{timeliness:2s}_{collection:3s}.SEN3/{dataset_name}_radiance_an.nc'`

The `AbstractYAMLReader` splits this pattern on that same separator, but then uses `os.path.join` to join in back to a path. In doing this, the `os.path.join` will use the platform depending separator. So on Windows, the pattern shown above transforms to:

`'{mission_id:3s}_SL_{processing_level:1s}_{datatype_id:_<6s}_{start_time:%Y%m%dT%H%M%S}_{end_time:%Y%m%dT%H%M%S}_{creation_time:%Y%m%dT%H%M%S}_{duration:4d}_{cycle:3d}_{relative_orbit:3d}_{frame:4d}_{centre:3s}_{mode:1s}_{timeliness:2s}_{collection:3s}.SEN3\\{dataset_name}_radiance_an.nc'`

The code doing this is:
```            
file_patterns = [os.path.join(*pattern.split('/'))
                             for pattern in filetype_info['file_patterns']]
``` 
https://github.com/pytroll/satpy/blob/master/satpy/readers/yaml_reader.py#L99

When searching for files, the [`get_filebase` function](https://github.com/pytroll/satpy/blob/master/satpy/readers/yaml_reader.py#L66) splits on `"/"`, but this separator won't occur because of the `os.path.join` mentioned above. Hence the length of the split will always return 1, causing the function to only return the filename, whereas it should return the parent folder as well. 

The resulting [`fnmatch` attempt](https://github.com/pytroll/satpy/blob/master/satpy/readers/yaml_reader.py#L78) will always fail because its comparing:

`fnmatch('S1_radiance_an.nc', r'???_SL_?_??????_????????T??????_????????T??????_????????T??????_????_???_???_????_???_?_??_???.SEN3\*_radiance_an.nc')`

Personally I think that using `os.path.sep` in `get_filebase` should work, since the pattern is created/parsed by using `os.path.join`. On Windows it does work, I tested it for the OLCI L1 and SLSTR L1 readers. But @djhoese expressed some concerns about this:
https://github.com/pytroll/satpy/issues/366#issuecomment-404569222

I'm not familiar enough with SatPy's internals to understand the implications of such a change.

issue_title: Check why not all cells are plotted in M1 example
Conversion process should not rely on InstanceNumber
issue_body:
Problem 1: `InstanceNumber` is Type 2 attribute - it can be missing. As of current version of dicom2nifti, missing `InstanceNumber` results in no output produced.

Problem 2: There is also no hard rule that requires InstanceNumber to establish the spatial ordering of the frames. The only reliable way to sort instances and reconstruct volume is to sort them using `Image Position (Patient)` and `Image Orientation (Patient)` (also see related discussion in http://dclunie.blogspot.com/2013/10/how-thick-am-i-sad-story-of-lonely-slice.html), and this approach is currently not implemented in the tool.

Speaking for myself, until this issue is resolved, it is hard to seriously consider using dicom2nifti.

issue_title: Meta profiling does not work with Django 2.0 and higher
issue_body:
# Problem
In Meta profiling a `models.Profile` is used. Its `ManyToManyField` named `queries` is directly assigned a list of `SQLQuery` objects. This behaviour has been [deprecated in Django 1.10](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/dev/releases/1.10/#deprecated-features-1-10) and [removed in Django 2.0](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/dev/internals/deprecation/#deprecation-removed-in-2-0).
See actual implementation [here](https://github.com/django/django/blob/master/django/db/models/fields/related_descriptors.py#L528).

# How to reproduce
Set meta profiling ON by putting the following line in `settings.py`:
```
SILKY_META = True
```
Call any function or view with the profiling turned on
## Error
You get the following error:
```
2018-04-02 16:45:05 [3066] [ERROR] [silk.profiling.profiler::__exit__:40] Exception when performing meta profiling, dumping trace below
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/django/path/virtualenv/lib/python3.6/site-packages/silk/middleware.py", line 122, in _process_response
    collector.finalise()
  File "/django/path/virtualenv/lib/python3.6/site-packages/silk/collector.py", line 183, in finalise
    profile.queries = profile_query_models
  File "/django/path/virtualenv/lib/python3.6/site-packages/django/db/models/fields/related_descriptors.py", line 509, in __set__
    % self._get_set_deprecation_msg_params(),
TypeError: Direct assignment to the forward side of a many-to-many set is prohibited. Use queries.set() instead.
```

# Proposed solution
Change direct assignment with [`.set()`](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/dev/ref/models/relations/#django.db.models.fields.related.RelatedManager.set) method. I will open a PR shortly.
## Before
```
silk/collector.py", line 183, in finalise
    profile.queries = profile_query_models
```
## After
```
silk/collector.py", line 183, in finalise
    profile.queries.set(profile_query_models)
```

issue_title: Modify TensorBoard to support plugin_data as an optional field.
issue_body:
After PR https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/pull/11952 goes in, the `plugin_data` field will be optional instead of repeated (for the motivation on why we made this change, see the TensorFlow PR). 

After that change enters nightly TensorFlow (and TF r1.3), we should change this line in the event accumulator to read 1 single plugin data proto.
https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorboard/blob/da8db355b64a0954342a0af56fe6eaad2542a203/tensorboard/backend/event_processing/event_accumulator.py#L372

as well as a couple of other places such as this line in a test that indexes into plugin_data:
https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorboard/blob/cb929ed7653a3a58333c63ccaeab7a700c5448c6/tensorboard/plugins/histogram/summary_test.py#L81

Other than those lines, as @jart noticed, TensorBoard will still be able to read pre-existing protobufs that contain summaries, which is nice. We should also update TensorFlow master as well as internal logic to reflect this change.

issue_title: otGetNetworkName() isn't zero terminated under certain circumstances
issue_body:
If a network name is set that is exactly 16 bytes long, the result from `otGetNetworkName()` is no longer zero-terminated. This causes all sorts of problems.

There are a few ways to work around this. Change the internal structure where we store the network name to use 17 bytes instead of 16 is the most obvious solution. Another solution would be to treat the network name as an opaque 16-byte value, much like we do for the XPANID—but this makes the use of the network name rather inconvenient.

issue_title: [kw41z] OPENTHREAD_ENABLE_CLI_LOGGING no longer supported
issue_body:
The example platforms _kw41z_ and _efr32_ include a header `<cli/cli-uart.h>` and a call to a function `otCliLog` that are enabled when the macro `OPENTHREAD_ENABLE_CLI_LOGGING` is defined. 

It seems that the header nor the function no longer exist, per https://github.com/openthread/openthread/commit/da357701406a38a94478b7f8c6a90f09cded0166

However, `src/cli/cli_uart.cpp` still supports `extern "C"`, so should this header be recreated and where?

issue_title: Timeout makes the next request to the same domain fail
issue_body:
Heya.

Whenever a request fail with a socket.timeout, the following request to the same domain fails. Example:

```python3
import httplib2
h = httplib2.Http(timeout=3)
h.force_exception_to_status_code = True

head_1, cont_1 = h.request('https://httpbin.org/delay/60', headers={'cache-control':'no-cache'})
# head_1: {'status': '408', 'content-type': 'text/plain', 'content-length': 15}
# cont_1: b'Request Timeout'

# right after the timeout, request anything to the same domain
head_2, cont_2 = h.request('https://httpbin.org/delay/2', headers={'cache-control':'no-cache'})
# Instead of a 2 second delay, it will reuse the connection made on the previous request, making the response timeout / have the response of the previous request.

```

I'm using the latest httplib2 and python3
