issue_title: Purge old private key material
issue_body:
Related to #4634.

When we designed the lineage format with `/etc/letsencrypt/archive`, we thought

* sysadmins will *manually inspect* their new certificates before "deploying" them

* sysadmins might find something to dislike about new certificates and choose not to use them

* sysadmins might notice something to dislike about new certificates after they're deployed and reverse the deployment by reactivating an older certificate

We've since almost entirely abandoned the first two concepts (for example, the default gap between obtaining and deploying a cert, which I had originally conceived of as one week, has been reduced to zero and the same code paths that obtain certs normally immediately attempt to install them).

I'm not sure I've heard of any cases where sysadmins have deliberately used the third option, since if they dislike the new cert (which is rather rare), they will probably just go ahead and get an even newer cert.

It's not necessarily inappropriate to have, or have the option of having, some history just in case the third situation actually comes up in an unanticipated circumstance, but there is a security cost to having lots of key material preserved indefinitely, and that's the case specifically where clients negotiate non-PFS ciphersuites with servers, such as `TLS_RSA_WITH_AES_128_CBC_SHA256` or `TLS_RSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256`. In the last few years there has been a general deprecation of these ciphersuites in favor of (mostly) DHE and ECDHE ciphersuites.

The difference is pretty significant; in DHE and ECDHE, the client and server exchange ephemeral Diffie-Hellman parameters which are discarded after the session negotiation is complete. These parameters are used to derive the session key. It's not thought to be feasible to derive the session key from the ephemeral parameters without solving the discrete logarithm problem (or elliptic curve discrete logarithm problem, in the case of ECDHE) and it's not clear that even powerful attackers will ever succeed when the parameters are chosen appropriately. (Unfortunately they will succeed when the parameters are chosen inappropriately. :frowning_face:)

By contrast, in the non-DH RSA ciphersuites, RSA is used *directly* for key exchange: the client picks the session key and encrypts it under the server's RSA public key, then transmits this encrypted key over the wire. That means that the server private key that was in use in the course of that key exchange can be used directly to recover the session key; indeed, Wireshark has a feature that does exactly this if you give it a server private key. (The way this breaks with PFS ciphersuites is something that has really annoyed people who are trying to do legitimate consensual network debugging of TLS-wrapped protocols, and I assume also really annoyed wiretappers.)

Our `/etc/letsencrypt/archive` directory is a useful target for anyone who wants to read old non-PFS TLS traffic with a particular server known to be running Certbot. If they can get ahold its contents, even years later, they can just go back to the appropriate 60-day period and plug the key into Wireshark and read any non-PFS HTTPS traffic they've intercepted.

The best remedy for this is to get more use of PFS ciphersuites, but I'm concerned that they only represent the majority of TLS traffic today, not the overwhelming majority, and hence there would still be potentially significant value for an attacker to try to compromise a machine to steal its old key material, that we helpfully maintain (in duplicate) in `/etc/letsencrypt/archive` and `/etc/letsencrypt/keys`.

I believe that old private keys should be purged, as best we can, on some schedule, in order to reduce the risk to old non-PFS TLS session contents. Maybe not immediately after renewal, but maybe after a second successful renewal, for example. It's not perfect, but we can `shred -u` the key file or something, creating a low probability that its complete contents will be recoverable later, rather than an effective certainty.

This could also be a configurable option, which probably should be enabled by default (maybe `purge_old_privkeys = True` or something).

We don't even need to unlink the files if we don't want to invalidate lineages from the broken symlink point of view. We can use `shred` instead of `shred -u`, and then the file still actually exists, but its contents have been overwritten a couple of times.

issue_title: Set up Appveyor
Deprecate DEFAULT_API_VERSION
issue_body:
This is more of a poll at this point than anything. The existence of `DEFAULT_API_VERSION` in conjunction with the optional kwarg in `Salesforce` and `SFType` seem to be causing more trouble than it's worth. New adopters want to use the latest version (as they should). If `DEFAULT_API_VERSION` is changed, you risk breaking existing code if there have been any backwards incompatible changes made across all of the services exposed by simple-salesforce.

My proposal is to deprecate `DEFAULT_API_VERSION` and related optional kwargs and instead make them required arguments. Yes, the proposed change breaks backwards compatibility on two levels, but it's to alleviate future confusion and pain around this.

Would love to hear feedback on this prior to going ahead with the change.

issue_title: Make Secret a valid BalanceProof
issue_body:
For a mediated transfer ABC these messages will be exchanged:

```
A>B MediatedTransfer
B>C MediatedTransfer
C>A SecretRequest
A>C RevealSecret
C>B RevealSecret
B>C Secret
B>A RevealSecret
A>B Secret
```

After these messages are exchanged, neither B nor C have an up-to-date message with the current `transferred_amount` that can be used to close/settle the channel (related to issue #181), either a new DirectTransfer or MediatedTransfer needs to be exchanged from A>B and B>C to provide a new balance proof.

Making `Secret` the messages that do state changes usable as balance_proof removes the need for an additional transfer among these channels.
- [x] Add to the `Secret` message the fields required to use it in the smart contract (related to issue #43):
  - [x] `transferred_amount`
  - [x] `locksroot`
  - [x] `nonce`
  - [x] `channel`
  - [x] `signature`
- [x] Update the BalanceProof
  - [x] Instead of registering the secret itself, register the secret message and save it
  - [x] remove `hashlock_unlockedlocks`
- [x] ~Add decoding logic for the `Secret` message into the `NettingChannelLibrary`~

I hate to be the party-pooper  but I think there is a bug in the newest version (1.3.2) when voxelplan is generated into DICOM. 

The trip2dicom command gives the following error in the terminal: "WARNING: pytrip.vdx:Reading .vdx in 1.2 format: no CTX cube associated, setting pixel_size to 1.0." Can it be that the new fix for the patient ID is somehow only working for the .vdx and then pytrip doesn't find a matching .ctx and sets a default pixel_size value?

issue_title: Generate base data for Eclipse
issue_body:
I hereby open the issue on where to put my makeEclipse scripts and in which form.

Eclipse requires as an absolute minimum for calculation of base data 3 input files - a set of depth-dose distributions for each energy in water for a beam propagating in vacuum and two sets of lateral profiles (in X and Y direction resp.) for realistically simulated settings (meaning focused diverging beams and the whole BAMS modelled - or at the least a water phantom with WEPL equal to the BAMS in question). Here lateral profiles in air at multiple distances from the BAMS per energy step are required, where one has to be in the isocenter. 
All files have to be in the Eclipse w2CAD format, which is fairly well-described in a number of guidebooks. Unlike TRiP, all data for all energies are put in one single file (thus output is one DDD wCAD-file, one lateral w2CAD-file in x-direction and one in y-direction).

I therefore did two folders - a makeDDD and a MakeLateral. Each folder is structured very similar to makeTRiP, containing the necessary SHIELD-HIT input files with the energy string being replaced by XXX, a simple bash script to copy files to folders for each user-requested energy and chance XXX accordingly and run the MC code using generatemc and lastly for the post-processing a .py script which processes and fits the MC output data and spits out w2CAD-files.
The scripts and the MC input files are - like in makeTRiP - all inter-connected. 

For makeDDD, the .py script is very similar to the ddd.py of makeTRiP. Main difference is in the write-out of the input file. The values "FWHM1", "FWHM2" and "factor" are not required by Eclipse but I left them in the script, since it was hinted maybe they could still be imported at a later stage somehow, if desired.

For makeLateral, the .py script has been written from scratch but is fairly simple and probably anyone from this forum could have done it better or faster:-P It fits the lateral profiles with a Gaussian fit, scales the fitted values to be "large" enough for Eclipse to properly understand and from that generate the two w2CAD files. For now I assume lateral beam symmetry and the x-direction profiles equal to the y-idirection profiles but that can easily be changed. I am considering to introduce a "strip routine" to not use all values in the w2CAD-files - for now I just throw away the part of the data under a certain value (like less than 10^-4 or smth).

Let me empathize that the scripts are all simple so I don't know if code snippets posted here are necessary to understand the point of it all. The bulk of the work was to check and evaluate the date files, which has now been done.

issue_title: CLI: 'zhmc session create' incorrectly reuses existing session
issue_labels: [{'id': 429586299, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/zhmcclient/python-zhmcclient/labels/area:%20code', 'name': 'area: code', 'color': '207de5', 'default': False}, {'id': 429588767, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/zhmcclient/python-zhmcclient/labels/resolution:%20fixed', 'name': 'resolution: fixed', 'color': 'cccccc', 'default': False}, {'id': 429587708, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/zhmcclient/python-zhmcclient/labels/type:%20bug', 'name': 'type: bug', 'color': '84b6eb', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
### Actual behavior

When the `ZHMC_*` environment variables are set for a user on a HMC, and the zhmc CLI is used to create a session for a different user on the same HMC, the new session is not created, and the zhmc command reports success.

To reproduce, using the ensadmin user (who gets only itself when listing users) and the acsadmin user (who can list all users):

```
$ eval $(zhmc -h 10.11.12.13 -u ensadmin -p pw1 session create)

$ env | grep ZHMC
ZHMC_HOST=10.11.12.13
ZHMC_USERID=ensadmin
ZHMC_SESSION_ID=4ky72v0ru0y28f5sqlr98ub3rr3900hxwmbkml3xwvmcdzxi89

$ curl -s -k --header "X-API-Session: $ZHMC_SESSION_ID" https://$ZHMC_HOST:6794/api/console/users | json
{
    "users": [
        {
            "name": "ENSADMIN",
            "type": "system-defined",
            "object-uri": "/api/users/2e46cf9c-81a8-11e4-97fc-5ef3fcb27748"
        }
    ]
}

$ eval $(zhmc -h 10.11.12.13 -u acsadmin -p pw2 session create)

$ env | grep ZHMC
ZHMC_HOST=10.11.12.13
ZHMC_USERID=acsadmin
ZHMC_SESSION_ID=4ky72v0ru0y28f5sqlr98ub3rr3900hxwmbkml3xwvmcdzxi89

# Note: The session ID has not changed

$ curl -s -k --header "X-API-Session: $ZHMC_SESSION_ID" https://$ZHMC_HOST:6794/api/console/users | json | grep name
            "name": "ENSADMIN",

# Note: This uses the unchanged session ID for ensadmin, and the HMC correctly reacts to that.

$ unset ZHMC_SESSION_ID

$ eval $(zhmc -h 10.11.12.13 -u acsadmin -p pw2 session create)

$ env | grep ZHMC
ZHMC_HOST=10.11.12.13
ZHMC_USERID=acsadmin
ZHMC_SESSION_ID=42ii9dn119pkjs6x9cvwymqdygycev350oyw4mmiobmfvxk70p

# Note: The session ID has changed

$ curl -s -k --header "X-API-Session: $ZHMC_SESSION_ID" https://$ZHMC_HOST:6794/api/console/users | json |grep name
            "name": "ACSADMIN",
            "name": "ADVANCED",
            "name": "ENSADMIN",
            "name": "ENSOPERATOR",
            "name": "OPERATOR",
            "name": "SERVICE",
            "name": "SYSPROG",
            . . .

# Note: This uses the new session ID for acsadmin, and the HMC correctly reacts to that.
```

### Expected behavior

`zhmc session create` should unconditionally create a new session, even when `ZHMC_*` environment variables are set.

### Execution environment

* zhmcclient version: 0.16.1.dev15
* Operating system (type+version): RHEL 7.3
* HMC version: 2.13.1
* CPC version: 2.13.1

issue_title: find(object-id) performs needless loop of "Get {Resource} Properties" operations
issue_body:
### Actual behavior

For some resource classes (e.g. CPCs and Adapters), `find(object-id)` performs a "List {Resource}" operation (which provides the object-id in its list result), but in addition the "Get {Resource} Properties" operation is performed for each resource in the list result in order to get the object-id.

So far, I found this for CPCs and Adapters, but all object-kind of resource classes need to be investigated for this behavior.

### Expected behavior

The invocation of the "Get {Resource} Properties" operations is not necessary given that the list operation already returns the object-id properties, and should be eliminated.

### Execution environment

* zhmcclient version: 0.12.0.dev19
* Operating system (type+version): RHEL 7.3
* HMC version: 2.13.1
* CPC version: 2.13.1

issue_title: GNU extensions used in call to strftime break non-glibc systems
issue_body:
This line contains a GNU extension in the format string passed to `strftime` (specifically the `%-d` part). This causes the `timestamp` variable to be an empty string on non-glibc systems (like the Alpine Linux Docker images, which use musl libc).

Removing the hyphen modifier from `%-d` should be painless on glibc and non-glibc systems alike. Stripping leading zeros does not appear to be required by [RFC2616](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc2616#section-3.3) or [RFC822](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc822#section-5) (which seems to be the format used here). Could this possibly be removed for good?

https://github.com/capless/warrant/blob/3d6aa8c84841a1c7183f702ed7b4ad993c1ff37a/warrant/aws_srp.py#L170

issue_title: Add a fallback mechanism
issue_body:
Right now `LocalizedValue` falls back to the primary language if no translation for the current language is present. I think it's interesting if the user could specify a fallback chain. This could be in a per-missing-language fashion:

```
LOCALIZED_FIELDS_FALLBACKS = {
    'en': ['es', 'eu'],
    'es': ['eu', 'en'],
    'eu': ['es', 'en'],
}
```

For backwards compatibility, `LOCALIZED_FIELDS_FALLBACKS.get(lang, [PRIMARY_LANGUAGE])`.

Alternatively, the setting could be a function that is given the missing language and field info.

This could be a global setting or a field parameter.

issue_title: Datacube-created xarray.Datasets can't use .to_netcdf()
issue_body:
The datacube.load() function returns xarray.Dataset objects that contain attributes that can't be saved to NetCDF format by the member function.
Objects that can't be serialized: CRS object, flags_definition and spectral_definition dicts.
CF attributes that xarray doesn't expect when it writes them,as it wants to write them to match the data: e.g. units for time coordinates.
The dataset is structured to be easy to work with, so it is missing the CRS NetCDF data variables required by the CF conventions. This is only added when we write out files using Datacube's NetCDF writer.

issue_title: socket.bind() is a blocking call
issue_body:
...for UNIX domain sockets, since it has to do filesystem traversal.

And for non-UNIX-domain sockets, it also is if you pass in the host or port by name. So far we avoided this by making all the methods on trio.socket.socket only accept "pre-resolved" host/port combos. Which is a bit awkward; really the main motivation was that `bind` was logically a non-blocking call, so it can't call getaddrinfo, and then I made this the rule across-the-board to keep things consistent. But maybe it would be better to instead make `bind` async, and then drop the whole "pre-resolution" thing and if the user passes us an unresolved address, just silently resolve it for them.

issue_title: Figure out how to stop fighting with pytest-asyncio over fixtures
issue_body:
As noted in https://github.com/python-trio/trio/issues/27#issuecomment-344572212:
> speaking of pytest-asyncio module, it considers any async fixture to be run with asyncio. So if it pytest pytest_fixture_setup is run before pytest-trio one (not sure how pytest decide which module execute first, but that's what happened during my tests), this cause a crash. So right now pytest-asyncio is not compatible with this pytest-trio

Here's my suggestion: since fixtures already have to be explicitly decorated, it's not too onerous to ask the user to also explicitly say which kind of async fixture they want. So instead of writing
```python
from pytest import fixture

@fixture
async def whatever():
    ...
```

you'd write
```python
from pytest_trio import trio_fixture

@trio_fixture
async def whatever():
   ...
```

We should probably also continue to install a fixture hook, but make it just detect when someone has accidentally written `@fixture async def ...` so we can give an error message saying to use `@trio_fixture` instead.

We should also try to convince the pytest-asyncio folks to switch to this system (CC @Tinche). Especially if we are going to install a fixture hook that will randomly cause their thing to break and tell their users to use `@trio_fixture` instead :-)

issue_title: confusing error message with simple configs
issue_body:
Using this config:

```
config = Config(executors=[ThreadPoolExecutor()])
```

parsl startup logs this message to the logger:

```
$ ./c.py 
2018-07-11 08:04:42 parsl.config:66 [DEBUG]  Checkpoint period only has an effect with checkpoint_mode='periodic'

This appears to be in config creation, as it happens even without passing that config to create a DataFlowKernel.

issue_title: Add Syntax Validation for DTCs
issue_body:
Initial implementation has validation methods implemented for each element in a DTC. 

Using a YAML Schema tool such as [Yamale](https://github.com/23andMe/Yamale) we can offload syntax validation from the code, which should result in a more robust codebase.

Same as a compiler has 3 stages: lexical, syntactic and semantic analysis, this will result in schema validation splitted into 3 parts:
- Lexical Analysis ~ YAML Reader (The DTC is a correct YAML that is stored in a Dictionary)
- Syntax Analysis ~ Syntax Validation with a Schema (e.g. there's no `Type` in field with an incorrect value)
- Semantic ~ Validation in each class (e.g. `SourceDTC`) points to a correct DTC.

A schema would look like this for streaming schemas:

```yaml
Type: enum('ProductML:DTC:Streaming')
Version: enum('2018-03-01')
Description: string(min=1, required=False)
Name: identifier()
Identity: expression()
Time: expression(required=False)
Filter: expression(required=False)
Store: include('dynamodb_store')
DataGroups: list(any(include('data_group_variable'), include('data_group_identity', include('data_group_session'), min=1)

---

data_group_variable:
  Name: identifier()
  Type: enum('ProductML:DTC:DataGroup:Variable')
  Store: identifier(required=False)
  Filter: expression(required=False)
  Fields: list(include('field'), min=1)

data_group_identity:
  Name: identifier()
  Type: enum('ProductML:DTC:DataGroup:IdentityAggregate')
  Store: identifier(required=False)
  Filter: expression(required=False)
  Fields: list(include('field'), min=1)

data_group_session:
  Name: identifier()
  Type: enum('ProductML:DTC:DataGroup:SessionAggregate')
  Store: identifier(required=False)
  Filter: expression(required=False)
  Split: expression(required=False)
  Fields: list(include('field'), min=1)

field:
  Name: identifier()
  Type: data_type(required=False)
  Value: expression()
  Filter: expression(required=False)
  Atomic: bool(required=False)

dynamodb_store:
  Type: enum('ProductML:DTC:Store:DynamoDB')
  Name: identifier()
  RetentionDays: int(min=1, required=False)
  ReadWriteUnits: int(min=1, required=False)
  Table: ProductML_Test_Sessions
```

issue_title: [Deprecation with Tensorflow API] - Contributions Wanted
issue_body:
# Issue Description

We have a few deprecation warnings raised by Tensorflow that we shall address.
I will try to list them here and keep this post updated.

Feel free to add new ones that you found, I will keep this thread updated.
Please provide a minimal code to show the deprecation warning.

# How to Reproduce the deprecation warnings

## Solved issues:
<details>

### Conv1dLayer - [SOLVED] in https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/pull/17455

```python
import tensorflow as tf
import tensorlayer as tl

import warnings
warnings.simplefilter('always', DeprecationWarning)

x = tf.placeholder(tf.float32, (None, 100, 1))
input = tl.layers.InputLayer(x, name='in')

net = tl.layers.Conv1dLayer(input , shape=(5, 1, 32), stride=2)
```

Instructions for updating: `NHWC` for data_format is deprecated, use `NWC` instead
```

### DeConv2d - [SOLVED] in https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/commit/fdec18588d7f8b5f6383601f1030ed71f634d1c0

import tensorflow as tf

import warnings
warnings.simplefilter('always', DeprecationWarning)

Instructions for updating: Use the retry module or similar alternatives.

### ReconLayer - [SOLVED] in https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/commit/fdec18588d7f8b5f6383601f1030ed71f634d1c0

import warnings

issue_title: Migration to oauthlib community
issue_body:
Hi everyone,

Since @idan accepted the oauthlib community migration, as a team, we should list what do we need to progress as a true community. I suggest to start with a small list, and please anyone, feel free to participate by adding any suggestions :-) 

Define/Improve release process:
- [ ] : **Tagging**
Wrong releases numbers: we have **2.0.3** on `github's releases`, **2.0.5** in `__init__.py` and **2.0.6** on `pypi`...something wrong for sure
- [ ] : **Publishing**
I'd recommand to continue to use Travis for publishing, however we could define version directly by using environment variable `TRAVIS_TAG` (see example at https://github.com/thomsonreuters/bottle-oauthlib/blob/master/setup.py instead of our current hard-coded value: https://github.com/oauthlib/oauthlib/blob/master/oauthlib/__init__.py ). Also, I've seen @ib-lundgren is the actual publisher of the pypi package, he looks inactive since years, but dunno if it's an issue.  
- [ ] : **Documentation**
README needs to be updated with the updated badge for **travis-ci.org repository** URL (owner must enable it, AFAIK), and also add a badge for the **documentation build**, cuz it's failing since long time: https://readthedocs.org/projects/oauthlib/builds/6483131/
- [ ] : **Communication**
No objection to continue using github issues and [Google+](https://plus.google.com/communities/101889017375384052571), however it seems a bit outdated. Also, the #oauthlib IRC channel is empty, wondering if we could create a Slack room if interested?

Future, roadmap:
- [ ] : Do periodic bugs washes could be beneficial

Also, a quick round table could be great. I start the introductions, I'm currently working on a OAuth2.0 RequestValidator implementation with bottle, and I've never worked with OAuth1.0, nor Django nor Pyramid nor Flask. However, I'm trying to have a good knowledge around the RFC involved here (oauth2, introspect, revocation, jwt...). I have not started yet the OpenID integration, but it will come soon.

issue_title: Bug report/RFE templates too exhaustive
issue_body:
**Describe the bug**
The current templates used to create a bug report/RFE are too exhaustive. IMHO they should be easier to fill and avoid asking too much because that will make users (at least, me) to get bored while filling up and forget about it.

**To Reproduce**
Steps to reproduce the behavior:
1.  Try to create an issue
2.  Try to fill all the fields
3.  Get tired
4.  Abandon

**Expected behavior**
A clear and concise form with simple questions.

**Screenshots**
![screenshot from 2018-06-01 11-30-20](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/346758/40833795-34ab4f60-658f-11e8-8199-9eeae05bc1ea.png)

**Environment (please complete the following information):**
Any

**Additional context**
I know it simplifies filtering and get focus on the issue and while I think it is ok for support issues on 'enterprise software', for an opensource project it is too exhaustive.

issue_title: send() does not round-robin
issue_body:
I have 1x webserver, 1x json_rpc_server and 1x xml_rpc_server. I also have 3 workers. (everything runs on the same box)

When the worker's startup, they each have the same list of 3 possible sockets: 
         ["tcp://127.0.0.1:9005","tcp://127.0.0.1:9006","tcp://127.0.0.1:9007"]

they each iterate through the list possible addresses until zmq does not throw an exception (see https://github.com/rbucker881/spotify_test/blob/master/src/spotify_test/echo.py)

Once the worker successfully binds it performs a blocking read.


On the web server side... each web server connects to the three addresses. When a transaction arrives, the web server performs a send(). The send() round robins nicely.


However, if one of the echo workers is killed, then the send() blocks when the round robin finally gets back to that server.


if, however, the web server binds and the worker connects, then client is able to round robin.


The complete example code is here: https://github.com/rbucker881/spotify_test
